section.title: The Priests Begin Their Ministry
section.sectionRationale: On the eighth day, after the seven days of ordination, Aaron begins his actual priestly service, offering for himself and then for the people. The chapter climaxes the ordination narrative when the glory of the LORD appears and fire falls from His presence, confirming that His acceptance of the whole system just instituted.
section.thesis: On the eighth day Aaron offers sacrifices for himself and for the people exactly as commanded, and when he and Moses come out and bless the people, the glory of the LORD appears to all Israel and fire consumes the offering, confirming that God accepts this whole system of atonement He has established.
section.frameNote: Three movements: Moses instructs Aaron and the people to prepare their offerings; Aaron offers first for himself, then for the people; and the glory of the LORD appears with fire from His presence, met by the people's shout of joy.

units.label: Preparing the offerings
units.note: for today the LORD will appear to you

verses.text: On the eighth day Moses summoned Aaron and his sons and the elders of Israel.
He said to Aaron, “Take for yourself a young bull for a sin offering and a ram for a burnt offering, both without blemish, and present them before the LORD.
Then speak to the Israelites and say, ‘Take a male goat for a sin offering, a calf and a lamb, both a year old and without blemish, for a burnt offering,
an ox and a ram for a peace offering to sacrifice before the LORD, and a grain offering mixed with oil. For today the LORD will appear to you.’”
So they took what Moses had commanded to the front of the Tent of Meeting, and the whole congregation drew near and stood before the LORD.
And Moses said, “This is what the LORD has commanded you to do, so that the {{kavod|glory}} of the LORD may appear to you.”

units.label: Aaron offers first for himself
units.note: atonement for himself and the people

verses.text: Then Moses said to Aaron, “Approach the altar and sacrifice your sin offering and your burnt offering to make atonement for yourself and for the people. And sacrifice the people's offering to make atonement for them, as the LORD has commanded.”
So Aaron approached the altar and slaughtered the calf as a sin offering for himself.
The sons of Aaron brought the blood to him, and he dipped his finger in the blood and applied it to the horns of the altar. And he poured out the rest of the blood at the base of the altar.
On the altar he burned the fat, the kidneys, and the lobe of the liver from the sin offering, as the LORD had commanded Moses.
But he burned up the flesh and the hide outside the camp.
Then Aaron slaughtered the burnt offering. His sons brought him the blood, and he sprinkled it on all sides of the altar.
They brought him the burnt offering piece by piece, including the head, and he burned them on the altar.
He washed the entrails and the legs and burned them atop the burnt offering on the altar.

units.label: Then for the people
units.note: sin offering, burnt offering, and peace offering

verses.text: Aaron then presented the people's offering. He took the male goat for the people's sin offering, slaughtered it, and offered it for sin like the first one.
He presented the burnt offering and offered it according to the ordinance.
Next he presented the grain offering, took a handful of it, and burned it on the altar in addition to the morning's burnt offering.
Then he slaughtered the ox and the ram as the people's peace offering. His sons brought him the blood, and he sprinkled it on all sides of the altar.
They also brought the fat portions from the ox and the ram: the fat tail, the fat covering the entrails, the kidneys, and the lobe of the liver,
and placed these on the breasts. Aaron burned the fat portions on the altar,
but he waved the breasts and the right thigh as a wave offering before the LORD, as Moses had commanded.

units.label: The glory of the LORD appears
units.note: fire falls, the people shout for joy

verses.text: Aaron lifted up his hands toward the people and blessed them. And having made the sin offering, the burnt offering, and the peace offering, he stepped down.
Moses and Aaron then entered the Tent of Meeting. When they came out, they blessed the people, and the glory of the LORD appeared to all the people.
{{eish|Fire}} came out from the presence of the LORD and consumed the burnt offering and the fat portions on the altar. And when all the people saw it, they shouted for joy and fell facedown.

kavod.gloss: Glory, weightiness, the visible manifestation of God's presence. Moses promises the people that the LORD's glory will appear to them, and by the chapter's end that promise is fulfilled visibly, before the whole assembly, confirming that God accepts the sacrificial system just established.

eish.gloss: Fire, here fire that comes directly from the presence of the LORD to consume the offering on the altar. This fire is the visible sign of divine acceptance, the same altar fire that Leviticus 6:13 commands must never afterward be allowed to go out.

basic.context: After seven days of ordination, Aaron finally begins his actual work as priest on the eighth day. He offers first for his own sin, then for the people's, following the sin, burnt, and peace offerings exactly as commanded. When Moses and Aaron come out from the Tent of Meeting and bless the people, the glory of the LORD appears visibly and fire consumes the offering, and the people shout for joy and fall on their faces.
basic.hermeneutics: This narrates an actual, historical inaugural event in Israel's worship; it is not a repeatable pattern for Christian services today. Its abiding truth is that God visibly confirms and accepts the way of atonement He Himself established, and that even the priest who mediates for others must first deal with his own sin.
basic.originalLanguages: Moses promises that the kavod, glory, of the LORD will appear, and the chapter's climax delivers exactly that, a visible manifestation of God's presence witnessed by the whole assembly. The eish, fire, that comes out from the LORD and consumes the offering is the same fire that afterward must be kept perpetually burning, Leviticus 6:13, since it began as a direct gift from God Himself.
basic.typology: Aaron offering first for his own sin before he can offer for the people foreshadows the superiority of Christ, who, unlike Aaron, had no sin of His own to atone for. The fire falling from God's presence to consume the offering pictures divine acceptance made visible, anticipating the Spirit descending visibly at Pentecost to confirm the acceptance of Christ's finished work, Acts 2:3.
basic.christ: Hebrews notes that Aaron had to offer sacrifices first for his own sins before he could offer for the people's, but Jesus, our great High Priest, has no such need, since He is holy, innocent, undefiled, and separate from sinners, Hebrews 7:26-27. Where fire fell to confirm the old covenant's inaugural sacrifice, at Pentecost tongues of fire confirmed the acceptance of Christ's once for all sacrifice and the outpouring of His Spirit.
basic.god: God visibly confirms His acceptance of the very system of atonement He designed, showing that He is not a distant deity indifferent to whether His people's worship is genuine, but One who draws near and makes His presence known. His glory appearing only after atonement was fully made teaches that access to His visible presence follows, rather than precedes, the dealing with sin.
basic.crossReferences: Hebrews 7:26-27 contrasts Aaron's need to offer for his own sins first with Christ's sinless priesthood. Acts 2:3 describes tongues of fire at Pentecost, confirming the Spirit's outpouring after Christ's finished work. Exodus 40:34-35 describes the glory of the LORD filling the tabernacle, the same glory that now appears to the whole people here.
basic.oneStory: This chapter shows God visibly confirming the sacrificial system He has just established, appearing in glory and sending fire to consume the offering only after atonement is fully made for priest and people alike. This anticipates Christ, the sinless High Priest who needed no atonement for Himself, and the Spirit's visible confirmation of His finished, once for all sacrifice.

context.literary: This chapter completes the ordination narrative begun in chapter eight, moving from the seven days of consecration to the actual, functioning start of priestly ministry on the eighth day, a day often associated in Scripture with new beginnings.
context.historical: This was the first time the sacrificial system commanded throughout chapters one through seven was actually carried out in full, publicly, before the entire assembled nation, making it the founding moment of Israel's ongoing worship at the tabernacle.
context.canonical: The pattern of fire falling from God's presence to confirm acceptance of a sacrifice recurs at key moments in Israel's history, such as Solomon's temple dedication, 2 Chronicles 7:1, and Elijah's contest on Carmel, 1 Kings 18:38, each echoing this founding moment.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To narrate the actual inauguration of Israel's priestly ministry, showing Aaron offering first for his own sin and then for the people's, and God visibly confirming His acceptance of the whole system with His glory and consuming fire.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This narrates a historical, one time inaugural event; it does not prescribe a repeatable ritual for the church. Its abiding principle is that God confirms and accepts the way of atonement He Himself provides, and that access to His visible presence and blessing follows genuine dealing with sin.

debates.question: Why must Aaron offer a sin offering for himself before he can offer for the people, verses 7 to 8?

views.label: Aaron, like every human priest, was himself a sinner
views.case: The order teaches plainly that no human mediator, however consecrated, is without sin; Aaron must be personally cleansed before he can represent others before God, a point Hebrews later develops at length.

views.label: A pattern establishing the sequence for every subsequent priest
views.case: Others note this sequence, self first, then others, becomes the standing order of service for the priesthood going forward, more a procedural template than a statement primarily about Aaron's personal sinfulness, though both points are compatible.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that the sequence highlights the priest's own need for atonement, a need Christ, the perfect priest, uniquely did not share.

study.originalLanguages: Moses' promise that the kavod, glory, of the LORD would appear finds its fulfillment at the chapter's climax, a visible manifestation confirming divine acceptance before the whole assembly. The eish, fire, that consumes the offering comes directly from the LORD's presence, marking the origin of the very altar fire that Leviticus 6:13 later commands must never be extinguished.

typology.type: Aaron offering first for his own sin, then for the people's
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who, unlike Aaron, had no sin of His own requiring atonement, Hebrews 7:26-27.

typology.type: Fire falling from God's presence to confirm the offering's acceptance
typology.fulfillment: Tongues of fire at Pentecost, confirming the Spirit's outpouring after Christ's finished sacrifice, Acts 2:3.

study.christ: Hebrews 7:26-27 draws the direct contrast this chapter sets up: Aaron had to offer first for his own sins before he could serve the people, but Jesus, holy, innocent, undefiled, needed no such preparation, since His own sacrifice was for others entirely, offered once for all. As fire fell from the LORD's presence here to confirm this founding sacrifice, tongues of fire fell at Pentecost to confirm the Spirit's outpouring following Christ's finished, once for all work.
study.god: God does not leave His acceptance of Israel's worship a matter of guesswork; He confirms it visibly, in glory and fire, before the whole assembled nation. Yet that glory appears only after atonement has been thoroughly made for both priest and people, teaching that God's manifest presence and blessing follow, rather than precede, the honest dealing with sin.

crossReferences.note: Aaron had to offer sacrifices first for his own sins, unlike Christ, our sinless High Priest.
Tongues of fire appeared at Pentecost, confirming the Spirit's outpouring after Christ's finished sacrifice.
The glory of the LORD filled the tabernacle, the same glory that now appears visibly to the whole people in this chapter.

study.oneStory: This chapter shows God visibly confirming, in glory and consuming fire, His acceptance of the sacrificial system He Himself established, only after Aaron has dealt honestly with his own sin as well as the people's. This anticipates Christ, the sinless High Priest who needed no atonement for Himself, and the visible confirmation of His finished sacrifice given at Pentecost through fire from heaven once again.

quiz.q: On what day did Aaron finally begin his actual priestly service, after ordination?

options.text: The eighth day.
options.note: Verse 1 explicitly states this timing, following the seven days of ordination described in chapter 8.

options.text: The first day.
options.note: The text specifies the eighth day, after seven full days of ordination had been completed.

options.text: The fortieth day.
options.note: Forty days describes other periods in the Torah, not this specific timing given in verse 1.

options.text: No specific day is given.
options.note: Verse 1 explicitly names the eighth day.

quiz.q: For whom did Aaron offer the sin offering first, before offering for the people?

options.text: For himself.
options.note: Verses 7 to 8 explicitly describe Aaron offering the sin offering for himself before turning to the people's offering.

options.text: For Moses.
options.note: The text specifies Aaron offering for himself first, not for Moses.

options.text: For the elders of Israel specifically.
options.note: The offering was for Aaron himself, then afterward for the whole people, not the elders in particular.

options.text: No offering was made for Aaron personally.
options.note: Verses 7 to 8 explicitly describe an offering made for Aaron himself.

quiz.q: What happened after Moses and Aaron came out of the Tent of Meeting and blessed the people?

options.text: The glory of the LORD appeared to all the people, and fire consumed the offering on the altar.
options.note: Verses 23 to 24 describe this dramatic, visible confirmation of God's acceptance.

options.text: Nothing further happened; the ceremony simply concluded.
options.note: Verses 23 to 24 describe a dramatic and visible divine response.

options.text: The people were struck with a plague.
options.note: The chapter describes joyful divine confirmation, not judgment; that judgment scene belongs to the following chapter, involving Nadab and Abihu.

options.text: No response from God is described.
options.note: Verses 23 to 24 explicitly describe God's visible response.

quiz.q: How did the people respond when they saw the fire come out from the LORD's presence?

options.text: They shouted for joy and fell facedown.
options.note: Verse 24 describes exactly this response of joyful worship.

options.text: They ran away in terror.
options.note: The text describes joyful worship, shouting and falling facedown, not fleeing in terror.

options.text: They demanded Moses explain what had happened.
options.note: The immediate response described is worship, not questioning.

options.text: No response is recorded.
options.note: Verse 24 explicitly records the people's response.

quiz.q: What three offerings did Aaron present on behalf of the people in this chapter?

options.text: A sin offering, a burnt offering, and a peace offering.
options.note: Verses 15 to 21 describe Aaron presenting exactly these three offerings for the people.

options.text: Only a grain offering.
options.note: A grain offering was included, verse 17, but as an addition alongside the sin, burnt, and peace offerings, not the sole offering.

options.text: A guilt offering only.
options.note: The guilt offering is not among the offerings presented for the people in this chapter.

options.text: No offerings are described for the people.
options.note: Verses 15 to 21 give a detailed description of the people's offerings.

questions: Aaron had to deal with his own sin before he could mediate for the people. Where in your own life do you try to help or lead others spiritually before you have honestly dealt with your own need before God?

The people waited through seven days of preparation before the glory of the LORD finally appeared. Where in your life are you impatient for God to show up, when He may be preparing you through a process you cannot rush?

When the fire fell and the glory appeared, the people shouted for joy and fell facedown, both celebration and reverence together. Does your own worship hold both joy and reverence, or does it lean entirely to one side?

This whole elaborate system existed to confirm that God genuinely accepted the atonement made. Do you know, with confidence, that you are accepted by God through Christ, or do you still wonder if your worship or effort is enough?

Fire fell from heaven to confirm the very first sacrifice offered at the tabernacle. How does knowing that God has always confirmed and delighted in the way of atonement He provides change how you approach Him today?

translationNotes.note: The eighth day marks the day after the seven days of ordination described in chapter 8:33-35 were completed, and in Scripture often signals a new beginning, as with circumcision on the eighth day, Genesis 17:12.

So that the glory of the LORD may appear to you frames the entire chapter's ritual as building toward a specific, visible goal, fulfilled explicitly in verse 23, tying this event to the tabernacle's dedication in Exodus 40:34-35.

Fire came out from the presence of the LORD describes a direct, supernatural origin for the altar fire, distinguishing it from ordinary human fire and explaining why it was afterward commanded never to be extinguished, Leviticus 6:13.
